---
canvas:
  allowed_extensions:
  - pdf
  - png
  - jpg
  - jpeg
  grading_type: pass_fail
  group_assignment: true
  group_set: Project Groups
  points: 1
  published: true
  submission_types:
  - online_upload
  type: assignment
title: Lab 0 – VS Code + PlatformIO Setup
---

## Learning Goals

- Set up VS Code with PlatformIO as your development environment
- Understand the PlatformIO project structure and `platformio.ini`
- Build, upload, and monitor an ESP32 program
- Use the serial terminal for communication and debugging

## Why PlatformIO instead of Arduino IDE?

You are most likely already familiar with VS Code from your programming courses. **PlatformIO** is a VS Code extension that replaces the Arduino IDE with significant advantages:

| Feature | Arduino IDE | PlatformIO (VS Code) |
|---------|------------|---------------------|
| Code editor | Basic | Full VS Code (autocomplete, multi-cursor, git, etc.) |
| Build speed | Slow (recompiles everything) | Fast (incremental builds, cached) |
| Library management | Manual zip files | One-line install in `platformio.ini` |
| Multiple boards | Switch manually | Per-project config |
| Terminal access | None | Integrated terminal |

Arduino IDE is simple but becomes frustrating as projects grow. PlatformIO gives you a professional workflow with powerful tools while still being beginner-friendly. It’s the best way to learn embedded development and will be used throughout the course.



## Part 1: Installation

### Install the development tools
Run this command in PowerShell to install the development tools:
```powershell
irm https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cenmir/python-dev-installer/main/download.ps1 | iex
```


### 1. Install PlatformIO Extension

1. Open VS Code
2. Go to **Extensions** (Ctrl+Shift+X)
3. Search for **"PlatformIO IDE"**
4. Click **Install** — this installs PlatformIO Core (the CLI) and the GUI automatically
5. Wait for installation to complete (may take a few minutes on first run)
6. **Restart VS Code** when prompted

### 2. Create your first project

1. Click the **PlatformIO icon** (alien head) in the left sidebar
2. Click **"Create New Project"**
3. Fill in:
   - **Name:** `hello-esp32`
   - **Board:** `Espressif ESP32 Dev Module`
   - **Framework:** `Arduino`
4. Click **Finish** — PlatformIO creates the project structure

### 3. Understand the project structure

```
hello-esp32/
├── src/
│   └── main.cpp          ← Your code goes here
├── lib/                  ← Project-specific libraries
├── include/              ← Header files
├── test/                 ← Unit tests
├── platformio.ini        ← Project configuration
└── .pio/                 ← Build cache (auto-generated, do NOT commit)
```

The most important file is **`platformio.ini`** — it defines your board, framework, libraries, and upload settings.

## Part 2: Understanding `platformio.ini`

Open `platformio.ini`. It should look like this:

```ini
[env:esp32dev]
platform = espressif32
board = esp32dev
framework = arduino
```

This is the minimal config. Let's add the settings you'll need throughout the course:

```ini
[env:esp32dev]
platform = espressif32
board = esp32dev
framework = arduino

; Serial monitor baud rate — MUST match Serial.begin() in your code
monitor_speed = 115200

; Upload speed — faster upload to the board
upload_speed = 921600

; Serial monitor: echo what you type so you can see your own commands
monitor_echo = true

; Serial monitor: send data when you press Enter (not character by character)
monitor_filters = send_on_enter

; Libraries — add dependencies here (no manual downloads!)
lib_deps =
    robtillaart/AS5600@^0.6.1
```

### Key `platformio.ini` options

| Option | What it does | Example |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| `monitor_speed` | Sets baud rate for serial monitor | `115200` |
| `upload_speed` | Upload baud rate (faster = quicker uploads) | `921600` |
| `monitor_echo` | Shows what you type in the serial monitor | `true` |
| `monitor_filters` | Filters for serial input/output | `send_on_enter` |
| `lib_deps` | Libraries to install automatically | `robtillaart/AS5600@^0.6.1` |
| `board` | Target board | `esp32dev`, `seeed_xiao_esp32s3` |

`monitor_echo = true` lets you see what you're typing in the serial monitor — without it, your keystrokes are sent but invisible, which is confusing.

`monitor_filters = send_on_enter` buffers your input and only sends it when you press **Enter**. Without this filter, each character is sent immediately as you type, which doesn't work well with `Serial.readStringUntil('\n')`.

### Adding libraries

To add a library, simply add it under `lib_deps`. PlatformIO downloads it automatically on next build.

**Find libraries** at [registry.platformio.org](https://registry.platformio.org).

## Part 3: Write, Build, Upload

### 1. Write the code

Open `src/main.cpp` and replace the contents with:

```cpp
#include <Arduino.h>

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  delay(1000);  // Give serial monitor time to connect
  Serial.println("Hello from ESP32!");
  Serial.println("Type something and press Enter...");
}

void loop() {
  if (Serial.available()) {
    String input = Serial.readStringUntil('\n');
    input.trim();

    if (input == "hello") {
      Serial.println("Hi there! ESP32 is alive.");
    } else if (input == "info") {
      Serial.print("Chip model: ");
      Serial.println(ESP.getChipModel());
      Serial.print("CPU freq: ");
      Serial.print(ESP.getCpuFreqMHz());
      Serial.println(" MHz");
      Serial.print("Free heap: ");
      Serial.print(ESP.getFreeHeap());
      Serial.println(" bytes");
    } else if (input == "led on") {
      pinMode(2, OUTPUT);
      digitalWrite(2, HIGH);
      Serial.println("LED ON");
    } else if (input == "led off") {
      pinMode(2, OUTPUT);
      digitalWrite(2, LOW);
      Serial.println("LED OFF");
    } else {
      Serial.print("Unknown command: '");
      Serial.print(input);
      Serial.println("'. Try: hello, info, led on, led off");
    }
  }
}
```

### 2. Build

Click the **checkmark** (✓) in the bottom toolbar to compile your code. The first build takes longer (downloads toolchain). Subsequent builds are **much faster** because PlatformIO only recompiles changed files.

### 3. Connect and Upload

Connect the ESP32 to your PC using a USB-C cable.

![Pin-out](graphics/02_Lab0_PlatformIO_Setup.png)

::: {.callout-important}
Our ESP32 boards require you to hold the **BOOT** button (the right button in the picture above) during the connection phase. When you click upload, **hold BOOT** until you see the upload progress start, then release it. If you don't, the upload will fail with a connection error.

**Why?** The ESP32 has two modes: *normal* (run your program) and *download* (accept new firmware over USB). On boards with auto-reset circuitry, the USB chip pulls the right pins (GPIO 0 low + reset) automatically. Our DevKit boards lack this circuit, so you must manually hold BOOT (which pulls GPIO 0 low) to tell the chip: "enter download mode on the next reset." Once the upload tool connects and flashing begins, you can release the button.
:::

Click the **right arrow** (→) in the bottom toolbar to upload.

### 4. Open Serial Monitor

Click the **plug icon** in the bottom toolbar to open the serial monitor.

You should see:

```
Hello from ESP32!
Type something and press Enter...
```

Now type commands: `hello`, `info`, `led on`, `led off`.

::: {.callout-important}
The baud rate in `monitor_speed` (in `platformio.ini`) **must match** the value in `Serial.begin()` in your code. If they don't match, you'll see garbage characters. We use **115200** throughout this course.
:::

## Part 4: Serial Terminal Tips

### `monitor_filters = send_on_enter`

By default, the serial monitor sends each character to the ESP32 **as you type it**. This doesn't work well with `Serial.readStringUntil('\n')`, which expects a complete line. The `send_on_enter` filter buffers your input and only sends it when you press **Enter** — exactly what our code expects.

### `monitor_echo = true`

Without this setting, your keystrokes are sent to the ESP32 but **not shown on screen** — so you're typing blind. Setting `monitor_echo = true` displays what you type in the serial monitor so you can see your commands as you write them.

### Line endings

By default, PlatformIO sends `\n` (newline) when you press Enter. If your code expects `\r\n` (carriage return + newline), add to `platformio.ini`:

```ini
monitor_eol = CRLF
```

### Other useful filters

```ini
; Adds timestamps to every line
monitor_filters = time

; Decodes ESP32 crash stack traces into readable function names
monitor_filters = esp32_exception_decoder
```

### Resetting the board

Press **Ctrl+T, Ctrl+R** in the serial monitor to reset the ESP32 without unplugging.

## Tasks

::: {.callout-note}
## Checklist
1. [ ] Install PlatformIO in VS Code
2. [ ] Create a new ESP32 project
3. [ ] Edit `platformio.ini` — set `monitor_speed = 115200` and `upload_speed = 921600`
4. [ ] Write the "Hello from ESP32" program in `src/main.cpp`
5. [ ] **Build** the project (✓ button)
6. [ ] **Upload** to the ESP32 (→ button)
7. [ ] Open the **Serial Monitor** and verify "Hello from ESP32!" appears
8. [ ] Type `hello`, `info`, `led on`, `led off` — verify responses
9. [ ] Add `robtillaart/AS5600@^0.6.1` to `lib_deps` and rebuild — verify it compiles without errors
10. [ ] Upload and open the serial monitor — verify the full build-upload-monitor workflow
:::

## Submission

Upload a **screenshot** showing:

- Your VS Code with the project open
- The serial monitor showing the ESP32 responding to your typed commands
